description: Small bird very actively foraging alone in bushes/low tree in and around cattail marsh on northwest side of the preserve. While the bird was solo at all times during my observation, it gave off a distinct impression of smallness - I would guess chickadee-sized or smaller. The bird was overall gray, the underparts very plain and slightly paler than upperparts. In some views there was a hint of buffiness at the throat and along the flanks, though a closer view may have revealed more buffiness. The breast, belly, and undertail coverts were pale and plain, with no real contrast between any of them. The bird had a thin, short, pointed beak, a thin, pale eyering, and slighly paler lores on an otherwise gray head. There was an identifiable rusty crown, though not extensive from the views I was able to get. The bird's upperparts were uniformly gray from the head to the tip of the tail, with the exception of a rusty rump that was visible when bird was moving and facing the opposite direction.Primarily based upon the (seeming) limited extent of the crown, my conjecture is this was a female, but given my lack of experience with the species, my inability for closer study of the crown, and lack of understanding of just how variable the extent of the crown can be on either of the sexes and at what ages, I am electing not to age or sex the bird in this report.This bird to my knowledge was originally found and reported on eBird by Cole, Beth, and Dave Sage on April 16, 2023, though I did not get a chance to chase it until the afternoon of April 17 after following numerous subsequent reports throughout both days. I looked for the bird for about an hour, and while I was investigating a separate bird noise emanating from the marsh to my west (likely a Song Sparrow, this bird never seemed to make noise while I observed it), this bird sallied from a low, snaggy shrub in the marsh. I got my binoculars up for a good look at it in the bush it had returned to, and then the bird popped up and perched for maybe 15-20 seconds atop the bush where I was able to get good, identifiable photos of it from my vantage. It was clearly gleaning some sort of insects from the bushes/air around the bushes while I observed it. It perused some more low shrubs on the far shore from me, working north and west, before ending up in a short tree for another 30 seconds or so, where I observed it moving through the interior and got a poor but identifiable photo of it's rufous rump. It then took off and flew quickly past a group of birders to it's north to land in some trees that bordered private property on the preserve's northwest boundary.
photographer: Photographer: Max Miller, Max Miller, Max Miller, Max Miller
